title: Multi-Head Attention
summary: Embedding dimension split across $h$ heads (not the sequence). Each head operates in $d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$ subspace with own Q/K/V projections. Outputs concatenated and projected through $W_O$ for cross-head mixing.
key_insights: Heads split the FEATURE dimension, not the sequence — every head sees every token
Each head can learn different relationship types (syntactic, semantic, positional)
Total computation same as single-head: smaller $d_k$ per head $\times$ more heads
$W_O$ after concatenation is the only place cross-head information mixes
Some cross-subspace interactions are lost — $W_O$ recovers them via linear combination

mathematical_core.reshape: $[B, n, d_{\text{model}}] \to [B, n, h, d_k] \to [B, h, n, d_k]$
mathematical_core.concat: $[B, h, n, d_k] \to [B, n, h \cdot d_k] = [B, n, d_{\text{model}}]$
mathematical_core.parameters: Per head: $W_Q^i, W_K^i, W_V^i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$. Output: $W_O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$

derivations.title: Computational equivalence: multi-head vs single-head

steps.name: Multi-head cost
steps.math: Per head: $O(Bn^2 d_k)$. Total: $h \times O(Bn^2 d_k) = O(Bn^2 \cdot h d_k) = O(Bn^2 d_{\text{model}})$ since $h \cdot d_k = d_{\text{model}}$

steps.name: Result
steps.math: Same $O(Bn^2 d_{\text{model}})$. Multi-head redistributes compute across parallel subspaces.

derivations.consequence: Diversity of attention patterns 'for free' in terms of compute.

derivations.title: $W_O$ recovers cross-head information

steps.name: Without $W_O$
steps.math: Output $= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{[B, n, d_{\text{model}}]}$. Each $d_k$-slice independently computed — no cross-head interaction.

steps.name: With $W_O$
steps.math: Output $= \text{Concat} \cdot W_O$. $W_O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$ mixes all $h \cdot d_k$ dimensions. Each output dimension = linear combination of ALL heads.

steps.name: Information recovery
steps.math: If $\text{rank}(W_O) = d_{\text{model}}$, any linear combination of heads is representable. Monitor: singular values of $W_O$.

derivations.consequence: $W_O$ is the synthesis layer. If many singular values $\approx 0$, cross-head info is being discarded.

derivations.title: What is lost by splitting into heads

steps.name: Setup
steps.math: Single-head: score uses all $d_{\text{model}}$ dims. Multi-head: each head uses only $d_k$ dims.

steps.name: Lost interactions
steps.math: If $Q$ dim 1 and $K$ dim $d_k+1$ interact, no single head captures it.

steps.name: Recovery
steps.math: $W_Q^i$ and $W_K^i$ are projections from full $d_{\text{model}}$ → $d_k$. They can learn to route cross-subspace interactions into a single head.

derivations.consequence: Loss is soft, not hard. Learned projections can route interactions, but must discover routings during training.

geometric_properties: Each head operates in a $d_k$-dimensional subspace — heads partition the embedding geometry
Monitor per-head rank of attention matrix $A$ — rank 1 = collapsed to single position
Singular values of $W_O$ reveal how many cross-head combinations are actually used
statistical_properties: Different heads should have different attention entropy distributions — identical = redundant
Monitor: average attention weight to [CLS] or first token per head — some heads 'park' on defaults
numerical_properties: Total attention params per layer: $4 d_{\text{model}}^2$ ($W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O$)
Reshape is zero-cost view operation — no FLOPs, no memory allocation
Memory: must store $h$ separate $[B, n, n]$ attention matrices

related: TRANSFORMER.FFN — different role: attention routes info, FFN transforms it

notes: For domain-specific applications, heads could be intentionally designed to capture known coupling patterns
Open direction: head pruning — some heads contribute little and can be removed
